perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, PCQ and ABW have executed that certain Co-Branding Agreement dated October 11, 1996, as amended on December 10, 1996 (as so amended, the "Agreement"); WHEREAS, the parties hereto desire to further amend the Agreement to modify the provisions of the Agreement regarding [***] of the term thereof; and WHEREAS, the parties hereto desire to set forth herein the terms and conditions of their agreements and understandings with respect to the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, PCQ and ABW have executed that certain Co-Branding Agreement dated October 11, 1996, as amended on December 10, 1996 (as so amended, the "Agreement"); WHEREAS, the parties hereto desire to further amend the Agreement and may modify provisions regarding [***] of the term thereof; and WHEREAS, the parties hereto desire to broadly outline terms and conditions potentially related to their agreements and understandings with respect to the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'desire to further amend' with 'desire to further amend and may modify,' which makes the intention to amend less definitive. Similarly, replacing 'to set forth herein the terms and conditions' with 'broadly outline terms and conditions potentially related to' introduces vagueness and uncertainty. This change makes the parties' obligations regarding the amendment and the clarity of the terms less certain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: By using ambiguous terms such as "may modify" and "potentially related to", the amended text suggests a less firm commitment to the specifics of the agreement. This can be interpreted as a failure to act in good faith, violating the UCC's implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contract performance, which requires honesty and reasonableness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by using terms like "may modify" and "potentially related to," making the commitment to the agreement's specifics less firm. This weakening contradicts the Uniform Commercial Code's (UCC) obligation of good faith, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1: "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." The change from 'desire to further amend' to 'desire to further amend and may modify' and 'to set forth herein the terms and conditions' to 'broadly outline terms and conditions potentially related to' creates a vagueness that undermines the good faith requirement of clarity and definiteness in contractual obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 3. To the extent, if any, that provision of this Agreement conflicts with or differs from any provision of the Agreement, such provision of this Second Amendment shall prevail and govern for all purposes and in all respects.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. To the extent that a provision of this Agreement appears to conflict with or differs slightly from any provision of the Agreement, a reasonable interpretation of this Second Amendment might prevail and could govern for some purposes.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text adds considerable ambiguity by changing definitive language to uncertain terms. Replacing "shall prevail and govern for all purposes and in all respects" with "a reasonable interpretation of this Second Amendment might prevail and could govern for some purposes" introduces subjectivity and limits the amendment's authority, making the resolution of conflicts unclear and dependent on interpretation. This weakens the contractual certainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: By suggesting that only a 'reasonable interpretation' of the amendment 'might' prevail and 'could' govern 'for some purposes,' the modification weakens the integration clause and opens the door for extrinsic evidence (parol evidence) to be used to interpret the contract. The original clause was intended to prevent outside agreements from superseding the written agreement. The modifications potentially allow less weight to be given to the written agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented (a) by course of dealing or usage of trade (Section 1-205 ) or by course of performance (Section 2-208 ); and (b) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity that undermines the original contract's intent to prevent outside agreements from superseding the written agreement, potentially allowing less weight to be given to the written agreement, as stated in the Law Explanation. The modified text introduces terms that might prevail and could govern for some purposes, which weakens the integration clause and opens the door for extrinsic evidence (parol evidence) to be used to interpret the contract. Snippet 1 states that terms which are set forth in writing as a final expression of their agreement may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement. The modified text weakens this by introducing ambiguity, thus contradicting the snippet. Therefore, the modified text contradicts legal standards on contract enforceability by making it uncertain which terms govern.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Except as modified hereby, the Agreement and its terms and provisions are hereby ratified and confirmed for all purposes and in all respects.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Except as generally understood, the Agreement and its terms and provisions are considered ratified and confirmed where applicable.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity through the phrases 'as generally understood' and 'where applicable'. This weakens the confirmation of the original agreement by making it subject to general understanding and applicability. The original text provides a clear and comprehensive ratification, while the altered version makes the ratification uncertain and conditional. This change introduces subjectivity, and relies on external understanding, and only confirms 'where applicable'.
perturbation.law_explanation: The phrase "as generally understood" implies that external understandings can influence the interpretation of the contract, which conflicts with the doctrine of integration. Integration requires the contract to be interpreted within its four corners. The 'where applicable' also introduces uncertainty and would require courts to determine under which conditions that ratification applies. This is contrary to the original intention of the contract clause.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity through the phrases 'as generally understood' and 'where applicable,' weakening the confirmation of the original agreement. The law explanation states that 'as generally understood' conflicts with the doctrine of integration, requiring the contract to be interpreted within its four corners. Additionally, 'where applicable' introduces uncertainty, requiring courts to determine the conditions of ratification, contrary to the contract's original intention. The altered text makes the ratification uncertain and conditional, introducing subjectivity and relying on external understanding, which contradicts the principle of clear and comprehensive ratification.
